conf-proc-title-group.conf-full-title: 2025 15th International Conference on Mathematics, Actuarial Science, Computer Science and Statistics (MACS)
conf-proc-title-group.conf-normalized-title: Mathematics, Actuarial Science, Computer Science and Statistics (MACS), 2025 15th International Conference on

conf-meta.conf-name: 2025 15th International Conference on Mathematics, Actuarial Science, Computer Science and Statistics (MACS)

title-group.article-title: An Intelligent Energy Management Model Integrating Renewable Energy and Smart Grids for Optimal Power Distribution

institution-wrap.institution: Department of Computer Science and Engineering
Maharishi School of Engineering & Technology, Maharishi University of Information Technology

abstract.p: The inadequacy of traditional power networks and domestic energy management systems to efficiently optimize energy use results in excessive costs and increasing carbon emissions. Real-time dynamic power distribution management is impossible with these systems, particularly when dealing with several renewable energy sources (RES). This research suggests an intelligent energy management (IEM) model that optimizes power distribution by combining smart grids, battery storage systems (BSS), and RES. The research aim is to reduce Power prices, peak-to-average ratio (PAR), and carbon ($\text{CO}_{2}$) emissions while ensuring user comfort and system stability. The IEM model employs energy scheduling and management strategies that include heuristic optimization methods, like Dynamic Walrus-Gazelle Optimization (DWGO), a hybrid optimization algorithm combining the Enhanced Walrus Optimization (EWOA) and Gazelle Optimization (GOA). The model optimizes energy consumption and storage based on real-time electricity costs, renewable energy availability, and load demand. The experiment uses Python 3.7.0 for implementation. According to numerical evaluations, power prices, PAR, and CO2 emissions have been significantly reduced respectively, in contrast to unscheduled. The suggested DWGO improves energy distribution while lowering costs and emissions and increasing system efficiency, making it a viable solution for reliable IEM models in smart buildings.

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Increasing world demand for power and the requirement to be sustainable have led to the integration of renewable energy resources (RES) into contemporary grid systems. However, the intrinsic intermittency of RES like solar and wind power is a reason for the problems of stable grids and the maximum delivery of power [1]. To combat the problems, smart grids developed to be more sophisticated with real-time sensing, predictive capability, and remote energy management [2]. A reliable energy management system utilizing renewable energy and smart grids guarantees optimum power supply, efficiency, and continuity [3]. The use of modern technologies like the Internet of Things (IoT), artificial intelligence (AI), and demand-side management makes the use of smart grids efficient in terms of dynamic load balancing and adaptive energy distribution. Energy storage facilities also facilitate the achievement of this integration by guaranteeing lower supply and grid vulnerability fluctuations [4]. It is predicated on the gathering of real-time data, forecasting energy supply and demand, and using optimization algorithms to achieve the most efficient energy flow balance. It also includes demand response strategies where customers consumption is adjusted based on the grid condition and dynamic price policies. The smart grid thereby enables more renewable energy to be used effectively while mitigating energy waste, lowering operational costs, and enhancing sustainability [5]. The constraints include high installation costs, cybersecurity threats, intermittent renewable energy feed, intricate infrastructure demand, data privacy issues, and the requirement for sophisticated forecasting and real-time control to enable optimal grid balance.

The objective of the DWGO is to provide optimal power supply by harnessing smart grids, energy storage units, and renewable energy systems, decreasing power price, PAR, and $\text{CO}_{2}$ emission, while ensuring system stability and user convenience.

sec.label: II.
sec.title: Related Works
sec.p: Through the integration of renewable energy sources and the implementation of a real-time price-based demand response program, the research [6] seeks to increase the efficiency of the smart grid. It suggests an intelligent framework for demand-side management that uses HGAC optimization algorithms and artificial neural networks. To improve smart grid sustainability, the experiment [7] aims to create an AI-based energy management system for SPV microgrids. Utilized a modified Grey Wolf Optimizer for optimal energy dispatch in grid-connected systems, which has been proven under various weather situations. Cost reductions of 23.34 % and 45.55 % were achieved on clear and overcast days, respectively. By combining battery storage and renewable energy sources, the evaluation [8] seeks to enhance energy management by lowering expenses, carbon emissions, and peak-to-average ratios. It suggests a controller for energy storage and load scheduling that makes use of heuristic algorithms. The objective of the evaluation [9] was to use Lyapunov optimization (LOT) to optimize energy in real-time in a smart house with an emphasis on thermal comfort and energy cost. The findings demonstrate a 20.15 % decrease in energy expenses while satisfying user needs. For smart grid energy management optimization, the research [10] created a hybrid genetic algorithm (GA) and enhanced differential evolution (EDE) called EDGE. Use EDGE in an Energy Management Controller (EMC) to react to demand response (DR) signals and plan residential loads. EDGE was superior to other methods in terms of reducing carbon emissions, energy expenses, the PAR, and user comfort.

sec.label: III.
sec.title: Methodology
sec.p:
The approach is to design an IEM model based on Dynamic Walrus-Gazelle Optimization (DWGO) that optimizes power allocation, energy scheduling, and storage concerning real-time cost, renewable supply, and load demand. Fig. 1 depicts the proposed system flow.
Fig. 1.
Flow model of proposed system

sec.label: A.
sec.title: Dynamic Walrus-Gazelle Optimization (DWGO)
sec.p: DWGO is a hybrid heuristic method that combines the DWOA and GOA to efficiently balance energy. DWGO utilizes walrus searching modes for global exploration and gazelle-evading mechanisms for exploitation. DWGO dynamically regulates energy scheduling in smart grids through the maximum use of renewable energy, battery, and grid dependency. This guarantees lower power prices, lower PAR, and lower $\text{CO}_{2}$ emissions, making it perfect for smart energy management systems.

sec.label: a.
sec.title: Dynamic Walrus Optimization Algorithm (DWOA)

sec.label: b.
sec.title: Gazelle Optimization Algorithm (GOA)
sec.p: Providing grid stability with reduced peak demand and carbon emissions. With the incorporation of GOA in the IEM model, there is real-time energy optimization, demand response, and load balancing. The algorithm significantly reduces electricity bills, peak demand, and $\text{CO}_{2}$ emissions, and hence it is a strong solution for smart grid energy management.

sec.label: B.
sec.title: Power Bills
sec.p: By lowering PAR, the technology gives a constant power supply, decreased peak demand, and improved energy efficiency.
The approach facilitates better energy usage in smart networks, improves sustainability, and reduces $\text{CO}_{2}$ emissions through energy schedule optimization.

sec.label: C.
sec.title: System Model
sec.p: The proposed system of the IEM model combines BSS, RES, and EG to maximize power distribution. It utilizes DWGO for real-time scheduling of energy.
The characters $\rho$ and $B_{s c}$ represent the turbine blade's surface and wind volume, correspondingly, while $U_{t}(s)$ represents the speed of the air in $m / s$.
This provides an uninterrupted power supply, improved energy utilization, and longer battery life in intelligent grids.

sec.label: II.
sec.title: Experimental Results and Discussion
sec.p: The experiment and effects of the DWGO were demonstrated. Windows 10 64-bit with a 2.80 GHz Intel Core i7-6700T CPU and 16 GB of RAM was used for the experiment. Three instances were examined: case 1 (EG): Depends entirely on the external grid for power. Case 2 (EG + RES) distributes electricity using both renewable energy sources and external grid power. Case 3 (EG + RES + BSS): Combines external grid, renewable energy, and battery storage for effective energy management. To assess whether the suggested scheduled algorithm and existing scheduled algorithms perform. The performance is compared based on a 24-hour evaluation.

Case1(EG only): IEM employs EG Electricity for scheduled and unscheduled (Unsc) loads. It addresses reducing the power price, PAR, and $\text{CO}_{2}$. BFO, and HGPDO significantly lower power bill prices (52.54 %, and 58.69 %, respectively). However, the proposed scheduling technique achieves optimal price reduction. Table I displays the average 24-hour price of traditional methods compared to the proposed model. Fig. 2 illustrates the case 1 power price ($W h$) contrast. Unsc Power price ranges from an extreme of 1091 cents in hour 9 to a lowest of 165.81 cents in 16 hours. In case 1, DWGO decreases the power price to 75.6 in 16 to 22 hours.
Fig. 2.
Case 1 comparison of power price (wh)
Table I. Case 1 contrast of power price
Algorithms Normal price (cents) Variance (normal cents) Decrease (%)
Unsc 337.34 −- −-
BFO [8] 160.07 177.2 52.54 %
HGPDO [8] 138.82 198.52 58.54 %
DWGO [proposed] 125.02 212.32 62.93 %

Table II and Fig. 3 show Case 1 PAR comparison, the unsc method has the highest PAR (3.302), which implies inefficient load balancing. BFO, HGPDO, and the proposed DWGO significantly decrease PAR with the lowest value (0.753) for DWGO a 77.19 % decrease, which reflects its potential for reducing energy distribution waste and peak load. Table III and Fig. 3 show $\text{CO}_{2}$ emission comparison, the desired $\text{CO}_{2}$ content (D) is 760 ppm. The unsc method increases $\text{CO}_{2}$ emissions (1014 ppm). Emissions are minimized by optimization algorithms, DWGO minimizes the minimum (64.5 ppm) by 91.44 %. This identifies DWGO's better result in reducing environmental load via effective energy management.
Table II. Case 1 comparison of par.
Algorithms Overall PAR Variance (PAR) Decrease (%)
Unsc 3.302 −- −-
BFO [8] 2.654 0.648 19.62 %
HGPDO [8] 1.559 1.743 52.78 %
DWGO [proposed] 0.753 2.549 77.19 %
Table III. Case 1 contrast of $\text{co}_{2}$ emission
Algorithms $\mathbf{C O}_{\mathbf{2}}$ emission(ppm) Variance(ppm) Decrease(%)
$\text{CO}_{2}$ desired 760 −- (Increased)
Unsc 1014.0 −254 −33.42%
BFO [8] 534.8 225.2 29.63%
HGPDO [8] 72.4 687.6 72.40%
DWGO [proposed] 64.5 695.5 91.44%
Fig. 3.
case 1 (a) par and (b) $\text{co}_{2}$ emission

Case 2 (EG and RES combination): The IEM uses both external grid power and RES for scheduled and unsc tasks. BFO, HGPDO, and DWGO significantly lower power prices by $25.27 \%, 47.55 \%$, and 51.20 %, respectively. In case 2, the suggested DWGO scheduling method achieves the best cost-reduction results. Table IV compares the average cost of all methods over 24 hours, including the suggested approach in example 2. The electricity price (Wh) contrast in case 1 is shown in Fig. 4. The Unsc price of Power ranges from a peak of 722.35 cents in hour 9 to a minimum of 109.10 cents in 16 hours. In casa 2, the power price was decreased by DWGO by 50.3 during 22 hours.
Fig. 4.
Case 2 comparison of power price ($w h$)
Table IV. Case 2 power price comparison.
Algorithms Normal price (cents) Variance (normal cents) Decrease (%)
Unsc 225.91 −- −-
BFO [8] 168.82 57.09 25.27%
HGPDO [8] 118.48 107.43 47.55%
DWGO [proposed] 110.24 115.67 51.20%

Table V and Fig. 5 (a) show case 2 PAR comparison, the unsc strategy possesses the largest PAR (2.985), which represents the worst load balancing. The algorithms perform better with BFO (26.80 %), HGPDO (45.02 %), and DWGO possesses the lowest PAR (0.851), a decrease of 71.49 %. This suggests DWGO's higher efficiency in optimizing energy distribution and minimizing peak load demand. Table VI and Fig. 5 (b) depict the $\text{CO}_{2}$ emissions for case 2. The desired carbon content (D) is 760 ppm. The unsc approach is connected to a higher emission of $\text{CO}_{2}(1003 \text{ppm}, 31.97 \%$ increase). Optimization techniques significantly minimize emissions, DWGO having the lowest (44.2 ppm) a 94.18 % decrease, reflecting DWGO better capability to promote sustainability through effective energy management.
Table V. Case 2 par comparison.
Algorithms Overall $\boldsymbol{P} \boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{R}$ Variance (PAR) Reduction (%)
Unsc 2.985 −- −-
BFO [8] 2.185 0.800 26.80 %
HGPDO [8] 1.651 1.344 45.02 %
DWGO [proposed] 0.851 2.134 71.49 %
Table VI. Case 2 contrast of co2 emission
Algorithms $\text{CO}_{2}$ emission (ppm) Variance (ppm) Decrease (%)
$\text{CO}_{2}$ desired 760 −- −-
Unsc 1003 −243 −31.97% (Increased)
BFO [8] 594 166 21.84%
HGPDO [8] 53.4 706.6 92.90%
DWGO [proposed] 44.2 715.8 94.18%
Fig. 5.
Case 2 (a) par and (b) $c o_{2}$ emission

Case 3 (EG combined with BSS and RES): The IEM in this case uses EG power for both scheduled and unsc tasks, in addition to RES and BSS. The total price of the power bill shows that BFO, HGPDO, and DWGO respectively, lower the cost by $29.72 \%, 33.66 \%$, and 43.72 %. However, in Case 3, the suggested scheduling approach, which evenly distributes demand between low and high peak hours, achieves the optimum price reduction. Table VII and Fig. 6. shows the average cost per twenty-four hours for all techniques and the proposed solution in Case 3. The unsc of power price rises during time hour 9, with the biggest electricity cost being 711.82 cents. Lowe's price was 102.88 cents in slotted hour sixteen. In case 3, DWGO power prices decreased by 45.2 in 23 hours.
Fig. 6.
Case 3 comparison of power price (wh)
Table VII. Case 3 comparison of power price
Algorithms Normal price (cents) Variance (normal cents) Decrease (%)
Unsc 222.51 −-
BFO [8] 156.38 66.13 29.72 %
HGPDO [8] 147.61 74.9 33.66 %
DWGO [proposed] 125.21 97.3 43.72 %

Table VIII and Fig. 7(a) show case 3 PAR comparison, the unsc method yields the maximum PAR (2.537), a measure of worst load balancing. The performance is enhanced with the assistance of optimization algorithms, wherein BFO (31.92 %), HGPDO (54.35 %), and DWGO yield the minimum PAR (0.821) an enhancement of 67.63 %. This shows DWGO's better capacity to optimize energy supply and peak loads effectively. Table IX and Fig. 7 (b) depict the $\text{CO}_{2}$ emissions for case 3. The Unsc method raises CO2 emissions ($968.3 \text{ppm}, 27.40 \%$ increase). Optimization methods reduce emissions considerably, DWGO reducing the lowest amount (52.3 ppm) by 93.11 %, showing DWGO's efficiency in reducing environmental load through optimized use of renewable energy.
Table VIII. Case 3 contrast of par.
Algorithms Overall PAR Variance (PAR) Reduction $(\%)$
Unsc 2.537 −- −-
BFO [8] 1.727 0.81 31.92 %
HGPDO [8] 1.158 1.379 54.35 %
DWGO [proposed] 0.821 1.716 67.63 %
Table IX. Case 3 contrast of $\text{co}_{2}$ emission
Algorithms $\text{CO}_{2}$ emission (ppm) Variance (ppm) Decrease $(\%)$
$\text{CO}_{2}$ desired 760 −- −-
Unsc 968.3 –208.30 –27.40 % (Increased)
BFO [8] 567.5 193.00 25.39 %
HGPDO [8] 63.5 696.5 91.64 %
DWGO [proposed] 52.3 707.7 93.11%
Fig. 7
Case 3 (a) par and (b) $c o_{2}$ emission

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The evaluation was intended to overcome the challenges of traditional home energy management systems and power grids, unable to optimize energy utilization, resulting in high carbon emissions and prices. It assessed an IEM system comprising smart grids, BSS, and RES for minimizing power distribution. The model utilized heuristic optimization methods such as DWGO. Numerical results revealed significant IEM decreases in power prices, PAR, and CO2 emissions in case 1, by $62.93 \%, 77.19 \%$, and 91.44 %, in case $2,51.20 \%, 71.49 \%$, and 94.18 %, in case $3,43.72 \%, 67.63 \%$, and 93.11 %, respectively, as compared to the unsc method. The DWGO-based IEM model proved to be capable of increasing the distribution of energy, minimizing expenses, lowering emissions, and increasing overall system efficiency, and thus it has effective potential for use in smart building energy management. This approach has limitations, including high startup costs, integration complexity, and reliance on weather-dependent renewable sources. Future plans include developing AI-driven predictive analytics, implementing blockchain to improve grid resilience and integrating energy storage technologies to provide sustainable and reliable power distribution in changing energy landscapes.

ref.mixed-citation: A. Kumar, M. Alaraj, M. Rizwan, and U. Nangia, “Novel AI based energy management system for smart grid with RES integration,” IEEE Access, vol. 9, pp. 162530–162542, 2021.

A. M. Abomazid, N. A. El-Taweel, and H. E. Farag, “Optimal energy management of hydrogen energy facility using integrated battery energy storage and solar photovoltaic systems,” IEEE Transactions on Sustainable Energy, vol. 13, no. 3, pp. 1457–1468, 2022.

K. M. Tan, T. S. Babu, V. K. Ramachandaramurthy, P. Kasinathan, S. G. Solanki, and S. K. Raveendran, “Empowering smart grid: A comprehensive review of energy storage technology and application with renewable energy integration,” Journal of Energy Storage, vol. 39, p. 102591, 2021.

E. Sarker, P. Halder, M. Seyedmahmoudian, E. Jamei, B. Horan, S. Mekhilef, and A. Stojcevski, “Progress on the demand side management in smart grid and optimization approaches,” International Journal of Energy Research, vol. 45, no. 1, pp. 36–64, 2021.

F. R. Albogamy, M. Y. I. Paracha, G. Hafeez, I. Khan, S. Murawwat, G. Rukh, S. Khan, and M. U. A. Khan, “Real-time scheduling for optimal energy optimization in smart grid integrated with renewable energy sources,” IEEE Access, vol. 10, pp. 35498–35520, 2022.

F. R. Albogamy, S. A. Khan, G. Hafeez, S. Murawwat, S. Khan, S. I. Haider, A. Basit, and K. D. Thoben, “Real-time energy management and load scheduling with renewable energy integration in smart grid,” Sustainability, vol. 14, no. 3, p. 1792, 2022.

H. W. Khan, M. Usman, G. Hafeez, F. R. Albogamy, I. Khan, Z. Shafiq, M. U. A. Khan, and H. I. Alkhammash, “Intelligent optimization framework for efficient demand-side management in renewable energy integrated smart grid,” IEEE Access, vol. 9, pp. 124235–124252, 2021.

A. U. Rehman, Z. Wadud, R. M. Elavarasan, G. Hafeez, I. Khan, Z. Shafiq, and H. H. Alhelou, “An optimal power usage scheduling in smart grid integrated with renewable energy sources for energy management,” IEEE Access, vol. 9, pp. 84619–84638, 2021.

P. Harsh and D. Das, “Energy management in microgrid using incentive-based demand response and reconfigured network considering uncertainties in renewable energy sources,” Sustainable Energy Technologies and Assessments, vol. 46, p. 101225, 2021.

Q. Hassan, P. Viktor, T. J. Al-Musawi, B. M. Ali, S. Algburi, H. M. Alzoubi, A. K. Al-Jiboory, A. Z. Sameen, H. M. Salman, and M. Jaszczur, “The renewable energy role in the global energy transformations,” Renewable Energy Focus, vol. 48, p. 100545, 2024.
